section.title: The Resurrection Body
section.sectionRationale: Paul now answers the practical objection: with what kind of body will the dead come? Using the picture of a buried seed, he shows that the resurrection body is both continuous with this body and gloriously transformed: sown perishable, raised imperishable; sown in weakness, raised in power; sown a natural body, raised a spiritual body. The last Adam, a life-giving spirit, gives us His own heavenly image. And at the last trumpet the perishable will be clothed with the imperishable, death will be swallowed up in victory, and the chapter ends not in speculation but in steadfast, hopeful labor.
section.thesis: The resurrection body is this body transformed, sown perishable, dishonored, weak, and natural, but raised imperishable, glorious, powerful, and spiritual; for the last Adam is a life-giving spirit who will refashion us in His heavenly image, so that at the last trumpet death is swallowed up in victory, and therefore we labor for the Lord knowing our work is not in vain.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the seed and the many kinds of bodies; sown perishable, raised imperishable; the last Adam and the man from heaven; and death swallowed up in victory, with the closing call to steadfast labor.

units.label: The seed and the many bodies
units.note: what you sow must first die

verses.text: But someone will ask, “How are the dead raised? With what kind of {{soma|body}} will they come?”
You fool! What you sow does not come to life unless it dies.
And what you sow is not the body that will be, but just a seed, perhaps of wheat or something else.
But God gives it a body as He has designed, and to each kind of seed He gives its own body.
Not all flesh is the same: Men have one kind of flesh, animals have another, birds another, and fish another.
There are also heavenly bodies and earthly bodies. But the splendor of the heavenly bodies is of one degree, and the splendor of the earthly bodies is of another.
The sun has one degree of splendor, the moon another, and the stars another; and star differs from star in splendor.
So will it be with the resurrection of the dead: What is sown is perishable; it is raised imperishable.
It is sown in dishonor; it is raised in glory. It is sown in weakness; it is raised in power.
It is sown a natural body; it is raised a {{pneumatikos|spiritual}} {{soma|body}}. If there is a natural body, there is also a spiritual body.

units.label: The last Adam, the man from heaven
units.note: we will bear the heavenly image

verses.text: So it is written: “The first man Adam became a living being”; the last Adam a life-giving spirit.
The spiritual, however, was not first, but the natural, and then the spiritual.
The first man was of the dust of the earth, the second man from heaven.
As was the earthly man, so also are those who are of the earth; and as is the heavenly man, so also are those who are of heaven.
And just as we have borne the likeness of the earthly man, so also shall we bear the likeness of the heavenly man.

units.label: Death swallowed up in victory
units.note: be steadfast, your labor is not in vain

verses.text: Now I declare to you, brothers, that flesh and blood cannot inherit the kingdom of God, nor does the perishable inherit the imperishable.
Listen, I tell you a mystery: We will not all sleep, but we will all be changed,
in an instant, in the twinkling of an eye, at the last trumpet. For the trumpet will sound, the dead will be raised imperishable, and we will be changed.
For the perishable must be clothed with the {{aphtharsia|imperishable}}, and the mortal with immortality.
When the perishable has been clothed with the imperishable and the mortal with immortality, then the saying that is written will come to pass: “Death has been swallowed up in {{nikos|victory}}.”
“Where, O Death, is your victory? Where, O Death, is your sting?”
The sting of death is sin, and the power of sin is the law.
But thanks be to God, who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ!
Therefore, my beloved brothers, be steadfast and immovable. Always excel in the work of the Lord, because you know that your labor in the Lord is not in vain.

soma.gloss: Body. For Paul the body is not a prison to escape but the good creation of God, to be redeemed and raised. The resurrection is precisely of the body, sown one way and raised another, so that the resurrection hope is not the survival of a disembodied soul but the raising and glorifying of the whole person.

pneumatikos.gloss: Spiritual, of or animated by the Spirit. The spiritual body does not mean a body made of spirit or immaterial; it means a body wholly enlivened and governed by the Holy Spirit, no longer subject to decay. Just as the natural body is animated by the ordinary soul-life, the resurrection body is fully animated by God's Spirit.

aphtharsia.gloss: Imperishability, incorruption, immortality. The quality of the resurrection body, which can no longer decay or die. The perishable must be clothed with the imperishable; what was subject to rot and death is remade to last forever, sharing the deathless life of the risen Christ.

nikos.gloss: Victory. The triumphant note on which the chapter ends. Death, the last enemy, is not merely survived but swallowed up in victory, its sting drawn out; and the victory is a gift, given to us by God through our Lord Jesus Christ, not won by our own strength.

basic.context: Paul answers the scoffing question: how can the dead be raised, and what sort of body would they even have? He points to a seed. You bury a bare grain, and God raises it as a living plant, related to the seed yet gloriously different. So it will be with our bodies. This same body is sown perishable, weak, and dishonored, but raised imperishable, powerful, and glorious. Christ, the last Adam, will give us His own heavenly likeness, and at the last trumpet death itself will be swallowed up in victory.
basic.hermeneutics: Paul uses everyday pictures, seeds and harvests, sun and stars, to teach a mystery that cannot be fully seen yet. He works by contrast: perishable and imperishable, dishonor and glory, weakness and power, natural and spiritual, earthly and heavenly. The point is both continuity and transformation. He grounds it in Scripture, quoting Genesis on Adam and Isaiah and Hosea on the defeat of death, and ends with a practical charge.
basic.originalLanguages: The key word is soma, body: the resurrection is of the body, not merely the soul. The raised body is pneumatikos, spiritual, meaning fully animated by God's Spirit, not made of vapor. Its quality is aphtharsia, imperishability, no longer able to decay or die. And the chapter climaxes in nikos, victory, death swallowed up and its sting drawn, a victory given to us through our Lord Jesus Christ.
basic.typology: The buried and rising seed pictures death and resurrection. Adam and Christ stand again as two heads: the first man of dust, the last Adam a life-giving spirit and the man from heaven, whose image we will bear. The clothing of the perishable with the imperishable answers the shame first covered in Eden, now swallowed up in the glory of resurrection.
basic.christ: Christ is the last Adam, the man from heaven, and a life-giving spirit. He does not merely receive resurrection life; He gives it. The image of the earthly man that we have borne will give way to the image of the heavenly man, Christ Himself, whom we will resemble. And it is through our Lord Jesus Christ that God gives us the victory over death.
basic.god: God is the one who gives each seed its body as He designs, and who will give us our resurrection bodies. He is the giver of the victory over death through Christ. The chapter shows a God who does not discard His creation but transforms it, clothing the mortal with immortality and defeating the last enemy, so that our labor for Him is never wasted.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 2 records the first man Adam becoming a living being, which Paul quotes and caps. Isaiah 25 promises that God will swallow up death forever. Hosea 13 asks where the plagues and sting of death are. Philippians 3 says Christ will transform our lowly bodies to be like His glorious body.
basic.oneStory: Here the Bible's hope for the body comes into full view. The dust to which Adam returned is not the last word; God raises and glorifies the body in the image of the man from heaven. Death, the ancient enemy, is swallowed up in victory, and the redeemed creation is remade imperishable. The story that began with a body formed from dust ends with that body raised in glory, like Christ's own.

context.literary: The section opens with a doubter's question in verse 35 and answers it in stages: the seed analogy and the variety of bodies in verses 36 to 41; the string of contrasts, perishable and imperishable, in verses 42 to 44; the Adam and Christ comparison in verses 45 to 49; and the climactic mystery of transformation and the defeat of death in verses 50 to 57, closing with the therefore of verse 58.
context.historical: The Corinthians, influenced by Greek disdain for the body, could imagine an immortal soul but stumbled at a raised body. Paul insists on bodily resurrection while distinguishing the resurrection body from mere flesh and blood as we now know it. His images of seeds, differing kinds of flesh, and the varied splendor of sun, moon, and stars would be readily grasped in that world.
context.canonical: This is Scripture's most detailed account of the resurrection body. It draws Genesis 2:7 into a new frame, fulfills the promises of Isaiah 25:8 and Hosea 13:14 that God will conquer death, and stands alongside Philippians 3:20-21 and 1 Thessalonians 4 in the New Testament's teaching on the transformation of believers at Christ's coming.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To answer how the dead are raised by teaching that the resurrection body is this body transformed, both continuous and gloriously changed, patterned on the risen Christ, and to move the church from doubt to steadfast, hopeful labor in light of death's coming defeat.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verses 35 to 57 are teaching about the nature of the resurrection body and the defeat of death. Verse 58 is the one imperative, and it is the goal of the whole chapter: because the resurrection is sure, be steadfast, immovable, and abounding in the Lord's work, knowing it is not in vain.

debates.question: What does Paul mean by a spiritual body in verse 44?

views.label: A body animated and governed by the Spirit
views.case: The contrast is not matter versus non-matter but two animating principles: the natural body is enlivened by ordinary soul-life, the spiritual body is fully enlivened and ruled by the Holy Spirit, glorified and deathless yet truly a body, as the risen Christ shows.

views.label: An immaterial or ethereal body
views.case: Some have taken spiritual to mean non-physical, but this collides with the seed analogy of continuity and with the bodily resurrection of Christ, who ate and could be touched.

debates.bearing: The orthodox reading holds that the resurrection body is real and physical, transformed and Spirit-animated, not a ghostly or immaterial existence; the hope is a glorified body, not the loss of one.

study.originalLanguages: Paul's answer turns on four words. Soma, body, keeps the hope firmly bodily: it is the body that is sown and the body that is raised, not merely a surviving soul. The raised body is pneumatikos, spiritual, which does not mean made of spirit but wholly animated and governed by the Holy Spirit, deathless and glorified, as the risen Christ shows. Its abiding quality is aphtharsia, imperishability, so that what once decayed is clothed with immortality. And the chapter crests in nikos, victory, as death is swallowed up and its sting drawn, a victory not achieved by us but given to us through our Lord Jesus Christ.

typology.fulfillment: Pictures death and resurrection: a bare grain is sown and dies, and God raises it transformed, as this perishable body is raised imperishable.

typology.type: The last Adam, the man from heaven
typology.fulfillment: Christ fulfills and surpasses the first Adam of Genesis 2:7; the first man became a living being, but the last Adam is a life-giving spirit whose heavenly image we will bear.
Fulfills Isaiah 25:8 and Hosea 13:14, God's promise to destroy death, accomplished through the risen Christ.

study.christ: Christ dominates the answer. He is the last Adam, over against the first: where Adam became a living being who returned to dust, Christ is a life-giving spirit, the man from heaven who bestows resurrection life. We have borne the image of the earthly man; we will bear the image of the heavenly man, Christ Himself, conformed at last to His glorious body. And the victory over death, so long promised, is His gift: thanks be to God, who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ. The resurrection body is nothing less than a body like His.
study.god: God is the giver throughout. He gives each seed the body He designs, and He will give us our resurrection bodies as He wills. He does not scrap His creation but transforms it, clothing the perishable with the imperishable and the mortal with immortality. And He is the one who gives us the victory over death through Christ, so that even now our labor for Him carries eternal weight and is never in vain.

crossReferences.note: The first man Adam became a living being, quoted in verse 45 and capped by the last Adam who is a life-giving spirit.
God will swallow up death forever, the promise fulfilled in verse 54, death has been swallowed up in victory.
Where are your plagues, O death, echoed in verse 55, where, O Death, is your sting?
Christ will transform our lowly bodies to be like His glorious body, matching bearing the image of the heavenly man.
At the trumpet the dead in Christ rise and the living are changed, parallel to verses 51 to 52.

study.oneStory: This passage brings the Bible's hope for the body to its height. The dust to which Adam returned is not the end; God raises the body imperishable, in the image of the man from heaven. The death that entered in Eden is swallowed up in victory through the last Adam, and the whole redeemed person, body and all, shares Christ's glorified life. So the chapter that began by defending the resurrection ends with a summons: because none of this is in vain, stand firm and pour yourself into the Lord's work.

quiz.q: What does the seed analogy teach about the resurrection body?

options.text: The body is both continuous with this one and gloriously transformed, as a plant is related to its seed.
options.note: Sown perishable, raised imperishable: continuity and transformation together.

options.text: The old body is discarded and a completely unrelated one appears.
options.note: The seed image stresses continuity, not a wholly unrelated body.

options.text: There is no body at all, only a soul.
options.note: Paul insists the resurrection is bodily, sown and raised.

options.text: The resurrection body is the very same body, essentially unchanged.
options.note: Right that it is the same body, but it is gloriously transformed, not unchanged.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by a spiritual body?

options.text: A real body fully animated and governed by the Holy Spirit, glorified and deathless.
options.note: Spiritual describes the animating power, not an immaterial substance.

options.text: A ghostly, immaterial existence with no body.
options.note: That contradicts the bodily resurrection of Christ and the seed analogy.

options.text: A radiant, glorified body unlike the present one in every way.
options.note: Close: it is glorious and transformed, yet it remains continuous with this body, as the seed shows.

options.text: The same weak, perishable body we have now.
options.note: It is raised imperishable and powerful, not left weak and perishable.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe the last Adam?

options.text: A life-giving spirit and the man from heaven, whose image we will bear.
options.note: Christ gives resurrection life and remakes us in His heavenly likeness.

options.text: The first man, formed from the dust of the earth.
options.note: That is the first Adam; Christ is the last Adam from heaven.

options.text: The risen Christ who shares resurrection life as all believers will.
options.note: He is risen, but more than a fellow recipient: He is a life-giving spirit who bestows resurrection life.

options.text: An angel who never took a body.
options.note: He is the man from heaven, truly human and risen bodily.

quiz.q: What happens to death according to verses 54 to 57?

options.text: It is swallowed up in victory, its sting drawn, and the victory is given us through Christ.
options.note: Death is defeated, and the victory is God's gift through our Lord Jesus Christ.

options.text: Death remains undefeated and always wins.
options.note: Paul proclaims death swallowed up in victory.

options.text: Death is defeated, and we share in that defeat by our own effort.
options.note: Right that death is truly defeated, but the victory is God's gift through Christ, not our achievement.

options.text: Death is merely ignored, not truly conquered.
options.note: It is genuinely conquered, swallowed up in victory.

quiz.q: What practical charge closes the chapter in verse 58?

options.text: Be steadfast and immovable, always abounding in the Lord's work, for your labor is not in vain.
options.note: The certainty of resurrection grounds steadfast, hopeful labor.

options.text: Withdraw from the world and wait passively for the end.
options.note: Paul calls for active, abounding work, not passivity.

options.text: Live for pleasure since the future is secure.
options.note: The call is to excel in the Lord's work, not to indulge.

options.text: Rest in the finished victory, since our effort now adds nothing.
options.note: The victory is indeed finished in Christ, yet Paul calls us to abound in the Lord's work, for it is not in vain.

questions: Paul insists the resurrection is of the body, not merely the soul. How does the hope of a glorified body change the way you view your physical life now?
The resurrection body is this body transformed, sown in weakness and raised in power. Where do you long for the day when what is perishable is clothed with the imperishable?
We will bear the image of the man from heaven, Christ Himself. How does the promise of becoming like Him shape who you are becoming today?
Death is swallowed up in victory, and the victory is a gift through Christ. How does that free you from the fear of death?
Because your labor in the Lord is not in vain, Paul says to be steadfast and immovable. What work for the Lord do you need to keep at, trusting it matters forever?

translationNotes.note: A spiritual body does not mean immaterial; the word describes a body animated and governed by the Holy Spirit, glorified and deathless, in contrast to the natural body enlivened by ordinary soul-life.

The first line quotes Genesis 2:7, the first man Adam became a living being; Paul caps it with his own inspired addition, the last Adam a life-giving spirit, drawing the contrast between the two heads of humanity.
Death has been swallowed up in victory draws on Isaiah 25:8, and the taunt where, O Death, is your sting echoes Hosea 13:14; Paul weaves both prophecies into his announcement of death's defeat.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 15:35-58. Any em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas to comply with the no-dash style rule; wording is unchanged.
